artifacts.content:
Erin,

Finally sitting down for five minutes while the kids are glued to their shows. I wanted to write this out so we’re on the same page about the sewer chaos and all the family stuff stacked on top of it.

First off, your mom: I’m still thinking about that December 3, 2024 mess at 1183 West Vine. The clay lateral there finally collapsed the same morning they discharged her from chemo at Memorial Medical Center’s oncology wing. Walking into raw sewage in her basement while she was immunocompromised was a nightmare. I’m glad we got the 6" PVC lateral and backwater valve in that day, plus the temporary bypass pump so she could at least use the bathroom safely.

Then there’s our own place. I keep flashing back to May 17, 2024 when the heavy rains flooded our basement and wiped out the little medical supplies fridge with your Springfield Clinic Rheumatology injections. I still can’t believe we’re paying about $1,900 a month out-of-pocket and fighting with that State Farm adjuster over whether the meds “count” as contents. No way I’m letting that happen again once I get the sump and backwater valve squared away.

On top of that, I’m trying to schedule around Daniel’s stuff. We’ve got his juvenile hearing at the Sangamon County Courthouse on March 12, 2026, and I absolutely have to be there with him and the public defender. So I can’t book any sewer inspections or big digs that morning.

I’ll sketch out a calendar this weekend—between his soccer, Sophie’s art night, your RA infusions, and everybody’s basements backing up, my head’s spinning.

Love you,
Mike

metadata.subject: Trying to line up sewer work, court dates, and family stuff

artifacts.content:
Personal business/financial notes – read before taking on any more big spring jobs.

1) Cash flow
I keep forgetting how tight things still are. Harrington Plumbing & Sewer, LLC has been on that $650/month IRS payment plan since August 2023 for the back payroll taxes when I misclassified that helper as a 1099 on a couple of Springfield jobs. It feels manageable month to month, but it really adds up when we’re slow and I’m fronting materials for 6" PVC runs and sump systems.

Need to keep at least one month of payroll plus that IRS payment in the account before I say yes to another big dig that requires saw-cutting driveways and renting extra equipment. No more “I’ll just float it on the card and hope for rain calls.”

2) Family timing
Dad’s prostate cancer surgery at Memorial Medical Center is scheduled for February 9, 2026. I told Mom I’d take at least three days off to be at the hospital with them and then help when he’s discharged back to their place on the west side. Block out Feb 9–11 (maybe 12) as NO JOBS that require me on-site all day.

3) Reputation / boundaries
Between Daniel’s vandalism mess, Karen’s sewer and alcohol stuff, and Laura’s backups, I keep bending over backward for “family emergencies.” Need to remember I’m still running a business that’s paying off an IRS mistake. Say yes to what I can, but stop promising miracle timelines.

4) To-do
– Call IRS to confirm remaining balance on payment plan.
– Ask accountant if there’s any way to accelerate payoff without killing cash flow.
– Update Carlos and Jay about February schedule so they’re not blindsided.

Hey Pat,

Figured I’d write instead of texting a novel. There’s a lot tied up with these sewer lines between Evergreen, Monroe, and College.

You remember when the city plumbing inspector, Mark Reynolds, came back out on June 14, 2024 about your old unpermitted basement bathroom at 746 Evergreen while you were still on probation? That’s when he flagged the illegal shared clay lateral between our place at 742 Evergreen Court and your rental next door. I’ve been meaning to disconnect and cap that old shared line and get each house on its own 6" PVC sanitary service. It’s been nagging at me every time I drive down the block.

On top of that, I’m still thinking about your duplex at 402 South College Street. The sewer separation and storm reroute there turned into way more drama than it should have once your Unit B tenant filed that formal complaint with the city in February 2024 over the sewage odors and that minor backup. When their stored tax records got damaged and they ended up with an IRS penalty notice, it stopped being just a plumbing problem.

All this is swirling around while I’m trying to keep my own house in order. Between the July 2023 backup into our playroom, Daniel’s asthma flaring when the basement stinks, and Sophie’s stuff getting soaked, I feel like I’m constantly one storm away from another disaster.

Anyway, I’ll let you know when I’m ready to trench on Evergreen so you can warn your tenants and move cars. Maybe we can grab a beer after and complain about tree roots and inspectors like old times.

– Mike

Event: Basement sewer + sump overhaul – HOME (742 Evergreen Ct)
Date: 2026-04-20 (tentative start – weather dependent)
Time: 7:00 AM – 5:30 PM (multiple days)
Location: 742 Evergreen Court, Springfield, IL – basement NW corner, driveway, front yard
Attendees: Mike, Carlos, Jay (crew)

Details / notes for myself:

– Scope: Full replacement of the existing 4" clay sanitary sewer service with 6" PVC SDR-26 from foundation wall to city main. Add new exterior cleanout. Separate footing drains from sanitary and install new sump pit and 3/4 HP pump in the northwest corner of the basement (Mom’s old bedroom area from after her May 9, 2023 stroke at St. John’s Hospital).

– History to remember: repeated spring storms in May 2023 backed sewage into that downstairs bedroom while Mom (Margaret) was staying with us and couldn’t manage the stairs to the upstairs bathroom. Then March–April 2024 we had more backups, including March 27, 2024 when raw sewage flooded the basement bathroom, Daniel slipped in it, cut his knee, and we had to take him to Springfield Clinic Urgent Care on Wabash for a tetanus booster and bloodwork.

– Also can’t forget the March 15, 2024 backup that flooded the finished basement, ruined Daniel’s bedroom carpet, and led to the $18,700 homeowner’s claim and the kids staying with Karen at 1180 West Vine for two weeks. That, plus the May 17, 2024 flood that destroyed Erin’s RA med fridge and those $1,900/month injections, is why this has to be done before next storm season.

– Scheduling constraints: avoid March 12, 2026 (Daniel’s juvenile hearing at Sangamon County Courthouse). Also block out Feb 9–11, 2026 for Dad’s prostate surgery at Memorial.

metadata.title: Plan: Home sewer & sump overhaul – schedule + history

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I wanted to catch you up on what’s been going on with the house and with you and Dad, since it all kind of ties together.

First, I still think about when you moved into the basement bedroom on the northwest corner here at 742 Evergreen Court after your mild stroke at St. John’s Hospital on May 9, 2023. Those May 2023 storms that pushed sewage back into that room scared the hell out of me, especially knowing you couldn’t get up the stairs easily to use the upstairs bathroom. That’s why I’m so focused now on separating the footing drains from the sanitary line and putting a proper sump pit and pump in that corner.

Then in April 2024, when Dad fell in our backyard and his walker leg punched through that soft spot over the sewer alignment, we found out the old 4" clay line had partially collapsed there. I’m replacing it with 6" PVC SDR-26 from the foundation to the city main, and I know that fall ended up in the paperwork for his long-term care insurance claim.

On top of that, the basement backups have hit everyone: Sophie’s carpet got ruined during the March 2025 mess, and some of Karen’s chemo supplies she keeps downstairs were contaminated. Daniel’s asthma has been worse since fall 2024 whenever the sewer gas smell creeps up, and his pulmonologist at Memorial warned us in October 2024 that this kind of thing can really hurt his lung function.

I promise I’m not just “playing plumber” at home. I’m trying to make sure the house is safe for you when you visit, for Dad when he’s here after his prostate surgery in February, and for the kids every day.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Why I’m tearing up the basement (again)

artifacts.content:
Running list – house + extended family sewer situations (so I don’t mix stories up in my head when I’m exhausted).

1) Our house – 742 Evergreen Ct
– July 2023: backup soaked lower-level playroom where Erin ran her in-home daycare for three neighborhood families; kids had to stay with Patrick on Monroe Street for three nights.
– March 15, 2024: big backup flooded finished basement, trashed Daniel’s bedroom carpet; $18,700 homeowner’s claim; kids moved to Karen’s at 1180 West Vine for two weeks.
– March 27, 2024: sewage in basement bathroom; Daniel slipped, cut knee; Springfield Clinic Urgent Care on Wabash for tetanus booster + bloodwork.
– May 17, 2024: flood ruined Erin’s RA meds fridge; $1,900/month shots from Springfield Clinic Rheumatology gone; still arguing with State Farm.
– Fall/October 2024: sewer gas making Daniel’s asthma worse; pulmonologist at Memorial warned about long-term lung function.
– August 2024: camera inspection found tree roots cracking 4" clay; prior backup ruined lockbox with old tax returns + copy of my Social Security card from the 2019 IRS audit of Harrington Plumbing & Sewer, LLC.

2) Karen / Laura – Vine Street
– Heavy April 2024 rains backing sewage into Karen’s basement at 1180 W Vine while she was in and out of Memorial.
– June 2024: IDPH citation at Laura’s in-home daycare at 1180 W Vine for sewage seepage near playroom floor drain; had to disinfect and camera-verify before reopening.

3) Parents – 530 N Maple
– 2010: I tied footing drains/sump into sanitary (illegal). Reverse-mortgage inspector flagged it March 2024; now correcting and rerouting.

4) Patrick – 402 S College
– Feb 2024: Unit B tenant’s complaint over sewage odors + minor backup that damaged their tax records and led to IRS penalty.

Keep this handy when talking to doctors, insurance, or family so I don’t mix dates or houses.

Thread: Mike ↔ Erin

Mike (7:42 PM):
Sophie finally crashed. I’m wiped. Can we please NOT have your mom staying here during the basement work this time?

Erin (7:44 PM):
Yeah, I was thinking the same. November 2024 was rough.

Mike (7:45 PM):
Exactly. Those two weeks after her inpatient alcohol detox at Gateway Foundation, with the hammering and fans running downstairs, totally set her back. And Sophie still talks about finding those hidden wine bottles in the laundry room. I don’t want construction noise and sewer smells on top of that.

Erin (7:47 PM):
I’ll ask Laura if Mom can stay with her when we start. Speaking of Laura, did you ever send her that update about the daycare situation?

Mike (7:49 PM):
Yeah. I reminded her that after the June 2024 IDPH citation at 1180 West Vine for sewage seepage near the playroom floor drain, we can’t cut corners. I’m doing a full emergency replacement of that broken sanitary service, disinfecting everything, and running a camera before she reopens.

Erin (7:51 PM):
Thank you. What about your parents’ place?

Mike (7:53 PM):
Got 530 N Maple on the list. I’m finally correcting those illegal footing drain and sump tie-ins I installed back in 2010 that the reverse-mortgage inspector flagged in March 2024. I’m not thrilled I caused that headache, but at least I can fix it.

Erin (7:55 PM):
You’re juggling a lot: your folks, my mom, Laura’s daycare, our basement, Daniel’s court date.

Mike (7:57 PM):
Tell me about it. I just want one year where the only thing in our basement is kids’ laundry and Sophie’s art projects, not sewage and inspectors.
